method.description: Register-resident WMMA throughput probe. Each wave (32 lanes) loads A and B fragments once from global memory, then spins for n_inner inner iterations of 4x4 = 16 back-to-back WMMA instructions (16x16x16 tile, wave32). No global memory on the hot path. One wave per block. Grid = n_blocks.
method.dispatch: hipLaunchKernelGGL, single hipStream, hipStreamSynchronize between timed launches.
method.timing: std::chrono::steady_clock, median of 5 timed launches after 3 warmup launches.
method.flop_counting_fp16: Each wmma_f32_16x16x16_f16 = 2 * 16*16*16 = 8192 FLOPs (MAC = 2 FLOPs). Per wave per inner iter: 16 WMMAs = 131072 FLOPs. Total = n_blocks * n_inner * 131072.
method.op_counting_int8: Same MAC convention as FP16: each i32_16x16x16_iu8 = 2 * 16*16*16 = 8192 OPs. Total = n_blocks * n_inner * 131072. Note: some vendors double-count int8 MACs as 4 OPs; we use the 2-OP convention to stay apples-to-apples with the FP16 number.

clocks_under_load.source: rocm-smi sampled during sustained probe run

power_w.source: rocm-smi Current Socket Graphics Package Power

fp16.note: Theoretical = 40 CUs * 1024 FP16 FLOPs/cycle * 2.9 GHz. 42% utilization is lower than the 9070 XT on the same kernel source; suggests RDNA3.5 WMMA issue rate or register-pressure ceiling on gfx1151 specifically. Worth a rocprofv3 SQ_WAVES + SQ_INSTS_VALU pass.

int8.note: INT8 only ~7% faster than FP16 on gfx1151. Same 4x4 register tile count and issue pattern — both are WMMA-issue-rate bound, not arithmetic-rate bound.

clocks_under_load.source: rocm-smi sampled during sustained probe run
clocks_under_load.note: rocm-smi sclk reading on gfx1201 appears to lag actual clock. Measured 148 TFLOPS FP16 requires ~2.9 GHz boost; official 9070 XT game-boost is 2970 MHz.

power_w.note: 50 W under a pure-WMMA probe is low for a 304-W TBP card — the probe is a narrow compute-only test, no VRAM traffic, so VRAM controller and front-end are idle. Don't read this as thermal headroom; a full GEMM would be much higher.

fp16.note: Theoretical = 56 CUs * 1024 FP16 FLOPs/cycle * 2.97 GHz. Peak reading above 100% is clock-sampling noise on a sub-2-ms launch; treat median 138 TFLOPS = 81% of theoretical as the real number. RDNA4 WMMA issue rate is materially better than RDNA3 on this exact probe.

int8.note: INT8 is 2.1x FP16 on gfx1201 — RDNA4 WMMA widens the INT8 path relative to RDNA3. On gfx1151 the INT8/FP16 ratio is only 1.07x, so this is a real architectural delta not a measurement artifact.

arch_comparison.note: Raw CU count ratio is 56/40 = 1.4x. Ryzen FP16 outperforms by 2.75x (clock ratio ~1.02x, so 1.94x is pure microarchitecture + issue rate). INT8 is 5.4x, entirely from RDNA4's wider INT8 WMMA path.

sanity_checks.fp16_strixhalo_in_range: Task spec predicted 28-36 TFLOPS; measured 50.2 TFLOPS is above. Recomputed theoretical using 1024 FP16 FLOPs/cycle/CU (RDNA3 WMMA spec) gives 118.8 TFLOPS peak, so 50.2 is 42% utilization — not 'wildly off', just the spec's theoretical was under-stated. 50 TFLOPS FP16 is consistent with AMD's own 8060S-tier WMMA numbers.
sanity_checks.fp16_ryzen_in_range: Task spec predicted 135-175 TFLOPS; measured 148 TFLOPS median is in-range. Peak sample of 178 is clock-measurement noise on a 1.5 ms launch.
sanity_checks.counting_convention_confirmed: Total FLOPs per launch = 1024 * 2000 * 131072 = 268435456000 = 268.4 GFLOPs. Divide by ms * 1e-3 * 1e12 to get TFLOPS. Spot-check: 268.4 GFLOPs / 5.35 ms = 50.17 TFLOPS. Matches.
